permanently_banned.reason: Cardiovascular risks including heart attack and stroke
permanently_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Contains ephedrine alkaloids that act as sympathomimetic stimulants, causing vasoconstriction, increased heart rate, elevated blood pressure, and cardiac arrhythmias. Linked to heart attacks, strokes, seizures, and sudden death.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned all dietary supplements containing ephedrine alkaloids (April 12, 2004)

permanently_banned.notes: First major supplement ban by FDA. Still found in some imported products. Over 16,000 adverse events reported before ban. Linked to 155+ deaths.

permanently_banned.reason: Cardiovascular risks and false marketing claims
permanently_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic amphetamine-like stimulant that constricts blood vessels and elevates blood pressure. Causes heart attacks, strokes, psychiatric effects, and sudden death. Not naturally occurring in geranium despite marketing claims.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned in dietary supplements (2013), WADA prohibited

permanently_banned.notes: Often disguised as 'geranium extract' despite not being naturally occurring in geranium. Linked to multiple deaths including military personnel. Still found in pre-workout supplements.

permanently_banned.reason: Severe cardiotoxicity, substituted in tejocote root supplements
permanently_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Contains cardiac glycosides (thevetin A and B) that disrupt heart rhythm, causing severe arrhythmias, heart block, and cardiac arrest. Also causes neurologic and gastrointestinal toxicity. Can be fatal even in small amounts.

regulatory_status.US: FDA Class I recalls ongoing (2023-2025), Import Alert active
regulatory_status.Global: Recognized as toxic plant, banned in supplements worldwide

permanently_banned.notes: FDA finalized testing November 3, 2025 identifying 26 products adulterated with yellow oleander. Many products purchased through or fulfilled by Amazon. Pattern shows deliberate substitution in tejocote root weight-loss products. 2022 incident: 23-month-old child poisoned. Multiple Class I recalls ongoing. Some companies refused recalls. Causes life-threatening cardiac toxicity - nausea, vomiting, dizziness, diarrhea, abdominal pain, cardiac changes, dysrhythmia, potentially fatal.
scientific_references: FDA Alert: November 3, 2025 - Final testing results and complete adulterated product list
CDC Report: September 2023 - Tejocote root substitution investigation

permanently_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic stimulant structurally similar to DMAA and amphetamines. Increases blood pressure, heart rate, and risk of cardiovascular events including heart attack and stroke. Not approved for use in dietary supplements.

regulatory_status.US: FDA determined not a dietary ingredient (2019). Not GRAS. Subject to enforcement actions.

permanently_banned.notes: Marketed as DMAA replacement. Found in pre-workout supplements. FDA issued warning letters to companies selling DMHA products. Not naturally occurring despite marketing claims.

permanently_banned.reason: Opioid-like effects, addiction potential, overdose risk
permanently_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Atypical antidepressant that acts as mu-opioid receptor agonist at high doses, causing opioid-like euphoria, severe physical dependence, and life-threatening withdrawal. Linked to overdoses, seizures, and deaths. Highly addictive.

regulatory_status.US_Federal: FDA warnings issued February 2024, May 2025. Not approved for any use.

permanently_banned.notes: Schedule I in Alabama, Utah. FDA issued multiple warnings 2024-2025. Linked to hospitalizations and deaths. Sold as 'gas station heroin' or 'za za'. Causes severe opioid-like withdrawal. Poison control centers report increasing tianeptine exposures.

permanently_banned.mechanism_of_harm: GABA-B receptor agonist that causes CNS depression, severe physical dependence, and life-threatening withdrawal syndrome similar to benzodiazepines and alcohol. Withdrawal can cause seizures, psychosis, and death. Highly addictive.

regulatory_status.US_Federal: FDA warnings issued 2019, 2023. Not approved for any use.

permanently_banned.notes: Schedule II in Alabama, Schedule I in Utah. Can cause life-threatening withdrawal. Poison control centers report increasing phenibut exposures. Sold as nootropic or anti-anxiety supplement despite serious risks.

permanently_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic amphetamine analog that increases blood pressure, heart rate, and cardiovascular risk. Structurally similar to amphetamine. Not naturally occurring in Acacia rigidula despite false marketing claims. Cardiovascular and neurological risks.

regulatory_status.US: FDA determined not a dietary ingredient (2015). Subject to enforcement.

permanently_banned.notes: Often falsely claimed to be from Acacia rigidula. Added to WADA 2024 prohibited list. FDA issued warning letters to companies. Not found in nature - purely synthetic compound.

permanently_banned.reason: Unapproved drug, does not meet supplement definition
permanently_banned.notes: Synthetic compound developed in Russia as pharmaceutical drug.

regulatory_status.US: Status pending review

permanently_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Potent mu-opioid receptor agonist with 13x higher potency than morphine. Causes respiratory depression, severe physical dependence, opioid-like withdrawal, and overdose risk. More potent than the parent kratom alkaloid mitragynine.

regulatory_status.US: FDA warning letters issued July 15, 2025 and July 29, 2025 to 7 companies. FDA announced July 29, 2025 intent to schedule as Schedule I controlled substance under CSA.
regulatory_status.DEA: Recommended for Schedule I classification by FDA (July 2025)
regulatory_status.States: Banned in states with kratom bans; additional state restrictions expected

permanently_banned.notes: FDA issued 7 warning letters July 2025 to companies marketing concentrated 7-OH products (tablets, gummies, drink mixes, shots). FDA announced intent for Schedule I classification July 29, 2025. Texas Poison Center Network: 192 exposure reports in 2025 (vs 107 in 2024, 122 in 2023). 7-OH shows 13x potency of morphine. Not lawful in dietary supplements or food. Louisiana kratom ban effective August 1, 2025.
scientific_references: FDA Warning Letters: July 15, 2025 and July 29, 2025
FDA Press Announcement: July 29, 2025 - Intent to recommend Schedule I
Texas DSHS Alert: August 2025 - 7-OH exposure surge

permanently_banned.reason: Increased risk of heart attack and stroke
permanently_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor that increases blood pressure and heart rate. Significantly increases risk of cardiovascular events including heart attack, stroke, and arrhythmias. Particularly dangerous for people with cardiovascular disease.

regulatory_status.US: FDA withdrew approval October 8, 2010. Illegal in dietary supplements.

permanently_banned.notes: Most commonly found adulterant in weight loss supplements (28% of tested products). Prescription drug withdrawn from market. Still frequently found in tainted supplements. FDA issues ongoing warning letters.

permanently_banned.reason: Thyroid cancer in animal studies (Delaney Clause)
permanently_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic coal tar dye that causes thyroid tumors in laboratory animals. Triggers Delaney Clause which prohibits any food additive shown to cause cancer in humans or animals. Also linked to hyperactivity in children.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned January 15, 2025. Effective January 15, 2027 for food/supplements, January 18, 2028 for ingested drugs. Banned in cosmetics since 1990.

permanently_banned.notes: Effective date Jan 15, 2027 for foods/supplements, Jan 18, 2028 for ingested drugs. Banned in cosmetics since 1990. Products manufactured before effective date can be sold until inventory depleted. California also banned under 2024 Food Safety Act.
scientific_references: Federal Register: January 16, 2025 - FDA ban order

permanently_banned.reason: Primary source of artificial trans fats, which increase cardiovascular disease risk
permanently_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Hydrogenation process creates trans fatty acids that raise LDL (bad) cholesterol, lower HDL (good) cholesterol, and increase inflammation. Significantly increases risk of heart disease, stroke, and type 2 diabetes. No safe level of consumption.

regulatory_status.US: FDA determined PHOs not Generally Recognized as Safe (GRAS) on June 16, 2015. Compliance deadline June 18, 2018. Final enforcement actions completed 2023.
regulatory_status.EU: Restricted to <2% trans fats in food
regulatory_status.WHO: Recommends elimination of industrial trans fats globally by 2023

permanently_banned.notes: FDA banned PHOs in 2015 with 3-year compliance period ending June 2018. Final administrative actions completed December 2023. Responsible for thousands of heart attacks and deaths annually before ban. Some products may still contain PHOs through extended compliance petitions.
scientific_references: DOI: 10.1056/NEJMoa1200303 - Trans fats and cardiovascular disease
Federal Register: June 17, 2015 - FDA PHO ban

permanently_banned.reason: Kidney failure, cancer, DNA damage - Class 1 carcinogen
permanently_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Potent nephrotoxin and carcinogen that causes irreversible kidney damage (Balkan endemic nephropathy, Chinese herbs nephropathy), urothelial cancer, and DNA mutations. Even small amounts can cause permanent kidney failure. IARC Group 1 carcinogen (highest cancer risk category).

regulatory_status.US: FDA Import Alert 54-12 (active). Completely banned in dietary supplements.

permanently_banned.notes: Class 1 carcinogen. Causes irreversible kidney damage and cancer. Found in traditional Chinese medicine herbs. FDA Import Alert 54-12 detains products at border. No safe level of exposure. Linked to thousands of kidney failure cases worldwide.

permanently_banned.reason: Liver damage, cancer risk from pyrrolizidine alkaloids
permanently_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Contains hepatotoxic pyrrolizidine alkaloids (PAs) that cause veno-occlusive disease (liver blood vessel blockage), cirrhosis, liver failure, and liver cancer. PAs are metabolized to toxic compounds that damage liver cells and DNA. Can be fatal.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned internal use (2001). FTC consent orders prohibit marketing for internal use. External use only permitted.

permanently_banned.notes: Contains hepatotoxic pyrrolizidine alkaloids. Banned for internal use in many countries. External use (topical creams) still permitted in some jurisdictions. Linked to liver failure and death. No safe level for internal consumption.

permanently_banned.reason: Unapproved SYNTHETIC drug ingredient, growth hormone-related compound, safety concerns
permanently_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic/recombinant IGF-1 is an unapproved drug that mimics natural growth hormone effects. Can cause hypoglycemia, joint pain, increased cancer risk (promotes cell proliferation), acromegaly-like effects, and cardiovascular complications. Prescription drug mecasermin (Increlex) is the only FDA-approved form.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned synthetic/recombinant IGF-1 in dietary supplements (1997). Only approved as prescription drug (mecasermin/Increlex). Natural IGF-1 from deer antler velvet remains legal.

permanently_banned.notes: SYNTHETIC growth hormone-related compounds banned in dietary supplements. Natural IGF-1 from deer antler velvet remains legal. Check source and synthetic vs natural distinction. Frequently found in bodybuilding supplements.

permanently_banned.reason: Drug ingredient exclusion rule - CBD is an approved drug ingredient (Epidiolex)
permanently_banned.mechanism_of_harm: While CBD itself has low toxicity, the FDA ban is based on drug exclusion rule (CBD was approved as prescription drug Epidiolex before being marketed as supplement). Concerns include liver injury at high doses, drug interactions (especially with blood thinners), and lack of quality control in unregulated products leading to contamination and mislabeling.

regulatory_status.US: FDA prohibits CBD in dietary supplements and food due to drug exclusion rule (2018). Approved only as prescription drug Epidiolex. Ongoing FDA warning letters.
regulatory_status.Congress_Nov_2025: FY2026 Appropriations Act (H.R. 5371, November 12, 2025) banned intoxicating hemp-derived cannabinoid products with THC caps.
regulatory_status.Executive_Order_Dec_2025: December 18, 2025 Executive Order directs new federal regulatory framework for hemp-derived cannabinoids including THC limits and CBD:THC ratios.
regulatory_status.State_Laws: Legal in many states for non-supplement uses, creating regulatory conflict

permanently_banned.notes: MAJOR 2025 UPDATES: November 2025 Congressional action (H.R. 5371) banned intoxicating hemp-derived cannabinoid products. December 18, 2025 Executive Order directs marijuana rescheduling (Schedule I to III) and new hemp regulatory framework. CBD cannot be marketed in dietary supplements under FDA drug exclusion rule. FDA rulemaking for NAC (precedent case) expected January 2026 - may impact CBD pathway. Safety concerns: liver injury at high doses, drug interactions, quality control issues.
scientific_references: H.R. 5371 (FY2026 Appropriations Act): November 12, 2025 - Hemp product restrictions
Executive Order: December 18, 2025 - Marijuana rescheduling and hemp framework

permanently_banned.reason: Beta-2 agonist with cardiovascular risks, WADA prohibited
permanently_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Beta-2 adrenergic receptor agonist similar to ephedrine. Increases heart rate and blood pressure, causes cardiac arrhythmias, palpitations, and potential heart attack or stroke. Particularly dangerous when combined with caffeine or other stimulants.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned June 2025. Warning letters issued to supplement companies.

permanently_banned.notes: Found in pre-workout and weight loss supplements. Can cause dangerous arrhythmias. WADA prohibited since 2017. Often derived from lotus seed embryo or aconite root.

permanently_banned.reason: Flame retardant chemical with potential thyroid and reproductive health risks
permanently_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Vegetable oil chemically modified with bromine (flame retardant element). Bromine accumulates in body tissues causing thyroid dysfunction, neurological effects, and potential reproductive harm. Animal studies show tissue damage and behavioral changes.

regulatory_status.US: FDA revoked authorization July 2, 2024. Effective July 3, 2024. Compliance deadline August 2, 2025.

permanently_banned.notes: FDA banned effective July 2024. Flame retardant chemical used as emulsifier in citrus-flavored drinks. Linked to thyroid dysfunction and reproductive issues in animal studies. Compliance deadline August 2, 2025. Previously used in Mountain Dew and other citrus sodas.
scientific_references: Federal Register: July 2, 2024 - FDA ban order

permanently_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Azo dye metabolized to aromatic amines that may be carcinogenic. Animal studies showed tumor formation. FDA determined could not establish safe level of use. Also linked to allergic reactions and hyperactivity in children.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned in 1976 - prohibited in foods, drugs, and cosmetics
regulatory_status.EU: E123 - permitted with restrictions and warning labels

permanently_banned.notes: One of the earliest FDA dye bans. Different from Citrus Red 2 (different chemical). Was widely used in foods/beverages before ban. Still permitted in some countries with restrictions. Named after amaranth plant but unrelated chemically.
scientific_references: FDA: 41 FR 41858 - Final rule banning FD&C Red No. 2 (1976)
DOI: 10.1016/0278-6915(94)90034-5 - Azo dye metabolism and toxicity

nootropic_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic compound (not found in nature or food) that is an unapproved drug in the US. While relatively low toxicity, it's not a dietary ingredient and has not undergone FDA safety review for supplements. Can cause anxiety, insomnia, agitation, and headaches.

regulatory_status.US: FDA determined not a dietary ingredient (November 25, 2019). Illegal in supplements. Ongoing warning letters.

nootropic_banned.notes: Found in supplements despite being unapproved drug. Only levetiracetam (Keppra) approved in US. Racetam class all banned in US supplements.
scientific_references: FDA: November 25, 2019 - Not a dietary ingredient determination

nootropic_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic stimulant nootropic with psychostimulant effects. More potent than piracetam. Can cause overstimulation, anxiety, insomnia, and cardiovascular effects. Not evaluated for safety in supplements.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned in supplements - unapproved drug

nootropic_banned.notes: WADA prohibited as stimulant. Not approved as supplement ingredient. Russian pharmaceutical used by athletes (banned).

nootropic_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Prodrug of modafinil (converts to modafinil in liver). Causes liver stress and potential hepatotoxicity with chronic use. Stimulant effects include insomnia, anxiety, elevated blood pressure. Not evaluated for supplement safety.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned in supplements - unapproved drug

nootropic_banned.notes: Discontinued in France 2011. Found contaminating piracetam supplements. DoD prohibited. Converts to modafinil (Schedule IV drug).
scientific_references: WADA: Prohibited list - Modafinil and prodrugs

nootropic_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Schedule IV controlled substance. Prescription drug for narcolepsy. Can cause serious skin reactions (Stevens-Johnson syndrome), psychiatric symptoms, cardiovascular effects, and abuse potential. Requires medical supervision.

regulatory_status.US: Schedule IV controlled substance. FDA-approved prescription drug (Provigil). Illegal in supplements.

nootropic_banned.notes: Schedule IV controlled substance. Found in nootropic supplements. Serious skin reaction risk. Prescription required.

nootropic_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic derivative of thiamine (vitamin B1) that is not a dietary ingredient. Unapproved drug in US. Can cause skin reactions, agitation, and tremors. Not evaluated for supplement safety.

regulatory_status.US: FDA warning letter April 15, 2025 to Gluten Free Remedies LLC. Not a dietary ingredient.

nootropic_banned.notes: FDA warning letter April 2025 to Gluten Free Remedies LLC. Synthetic thiamine derivative. Pharmaceutical in France/Japan.

nootropic_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic peptide nootropic (not found in nature). Unapproved drug with no safety evaluation for supplements. Can cause irritability, sleep disturbances, headaches, and elevated blood pressure.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned in supplements - not a dietary ingredient (2019)

nootropic_banned.notes: Found in all 10 tested nootropic supplements in 2021 study. Russian pharmaceutical. Widely sold illegally in US supplements.
scientific_references: FDA: November 25, 2019 - Not a dietary ingredient

nootropic_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic racetam nootropic (not found in nature). Unapproved drug in US. Can cause anxiety, insomnia, and headaches. No safety evaluation for supplements.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned in supplements - not a dietary ingredient (2019)

nootropic_banned.notes: Approved for dementia in Italy/Argentina/China. Found undeclared in supplements. Part of racetam class all banned in US.
scientific_references: FDA: November 25, 2019 - Not a dietary ingredient

nootropic_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic compound derived from vincamine (periwinkle plant alkaloid). Causes birth defects in animal studies. Can cause low blood pressure, bleeding risk, and immune suppression. Particularly dangerous for pregnant women.

regulatory_status.US: FDA determined not a dietary ingredient (October 11, 2019). Warning for women of childbearing age. Illegal in supplements.

nootropic_banned.notes: FDA warning for women of childbearing age due to birth defect risk. Pharmaceutical in Germany/Russia/China. Can cause miscarriage.
scientific_references: FDA: October 11, 2019 - Not a dietary ingredient determination

nootropic_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic adamantane derivative with stimulant and anxiolytic properties. Unapproved drug. Can cause overstimulation, insomnia, and cardiovascular effects. No safety data for supplements.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned in supplements (June 2025). Unapproved drug.

nootropic_banned.notes: Russian pharmaceutical. Found in nootropic supplements 2025. Previously WADA prohibited. Stimulant effects.

nootropic_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Research chemical with no human safety data. Beta-carboline compounds can be neurotoxic and cause tremors, seizures, and anxiety. Not evaluated for human consumption. Potential dopaminergic neurotoxicity.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned in supplements (July 2025). Research chemical, not approved for human use.

nootropic_banned.notes: Found in cognitive enhancement supplements. No human safety data. Research chemical only. Potential neurotoxicity.

nootropic_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Fluorinated analog of modafinil (Schedule IV drug). More potent than modafinil. Can cause insomnia, anxiety, cardiovascular effects, and potential abuse. No safety evaluation for human use.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned in supplements (August 2025). Unapproved drug. Analog of Schedule IV controlled substance.

nootropic_banned.notes: Found in nootropic supplements 2025. More potent than modafinil. Designer drug analog of controlled substance.

nootropic_banned.reason: Unapproved new drug; failed clinical trials; not approved for supplements
nootropic_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic racetam compound that failed Phase III clinical trials for ADHD. Unapproved drug with no safety evaluation for supplements. Can cause headaches, fatigue, and gastrointestinal issues.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned in supplements - not a dietary ingredient (2019). Failed clinical trials.

nootropic_banned.notes: Experimental drug. Failed Phase III clinical trials for ADHD. Not approved for dietary supplements. Part of racetam class.
scientific_references: FDA: November 25, 2019 - Not a dietary ingredient

nootropic_banned.reason: Unapproved synthetic compound; not approved for human consumption
nootropic_banned.mechanism_of_harm: Experimental research chemical with no human safety data. Never approved for any use anywhere. Potential for unknown adverse effects. Not evaluated for human consumption.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned in supplements - not a dietary ingredient (2019). Research chemical only.

nootropic_banned.notes: Experimental cognitive enhancer. Never approved for any use. Research chemical with no human safety studies. Frequently found in nootropic supplements.
scientific_references: FDA: November 25, 2019 - Not a dietary ingredient

sarms_prohibited.mechanism_of_harm: Selective androgen receptor modulator (SARM) - unapproved investigational drug. Causes liver toxicity, testosterone suppression, heart attack risk, and stroke risk. Failed clinical trials. Not evaluated for supplement safety.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned in supplements (October 31, 2017). Unapproved drug. Ongoing warning letters.
regulatory_status.WADA: Prohibited at all times (S1.2 Other Anabolic Agents)

sarms_prohibited.notes: Most common SARM found in supplements (39% of tested products). Linked to liver toxicity, heart attack, stroke. Failed Phase III trials for cancer cachexia.

sarms_prohibited.mechanism_of_harm: SARM that suppresses natural testosterone production, causes liver damage, increases heart attack and stroke risk. Unapproved investigational drug. Can cause hormonal imbalances and cardiovascular events.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned in supplements (October 31, 2017). Unapproved drug.

sarms_prohibited.notes: Frequently found in bodybuilding supplements (30% of tested products). Can cause testosterone suppression, liver damage, cardiovascular events.

sarms_prohibited.mechanism_of_harm: PPARδ agonist (not technically a SARM) that caused cancer in multiple organs in animal studies at all doses tested. Rapidly promotes cancer development in liver, bladder, stomach, skin, tongue, testes, ovaries, and thyroid. Clinical trials terminated due to cancer risk.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned in supplements (October 31, 2017). Clinical trials terminated due to cancer.
regulatory_status.WADA: Prohibited at all times (S4.5 Metabolic Modulators)
regulatory_status.Global: Clinical development abandoned due to cancer risk

sarms_prohibited.notes: Not technically a SARM but grouped with them. Caused cancer in multiple animal studies at ALL doses. Clinical trials terminated. Extremely dangerous.

sarms_prohibited.mechanism_of_harm: SARM that causes vision disturbances (yellow tint, night blindness, difficulty adjusting to darkness), testosterone suppression, and liver toxicity. Binds to ocular receptors causing vision problems. Unapproved drug.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned in supplements (October 31, 2017). Unapproved drug.
regulatory_status.Clinical_Status: Development discontinued due to vision side effects

sarms_prohibited.notes: Can cause yellow-tinted vision and night blindness. Development discontinued. Vision problems can persist after discontinuation.

sarms_prohibited.mechanism_of_harm: SARM with high anabolic potency. Causes severe liver toxicity (hepatotoxicity), testosterone suppression, cardiovascular risks, and potential kidney damage. Multiple case reports of liver injury requiring hospitalization.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned in supplements (October 31, 2017). Unapproved drug. Multiple warning letters for liver injury.

sarms_prohibited.notes: Marketed as testosterone replacement alternative. Reports of severe liver damage requiring hospitalization. One of the most hepatotoxic SARMs.
scientific_references: DOI: 10.14309/crj.0000000000000518 - RAD140 liver injury case report

sarms_prohibited.reason: Unapproved SARM; synthetic anabolic compound; not approved for human use
sarms_prohibited.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic steroidal SARM and myostatin inhibitor. Unapproved research chemical with no human safety data. Can cause liver toxicity, testosterone suppression, and unknown long-term effects. Structurally similar to DHT (dihydrotestosterone).

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned in supplements (2017). Unapproved drug.
regulatory_status.Global: No approvals anywhere. Research chemical only.

sarms_prohibited.notes: Myostatin inhibitor marketed as SARM. Banned from supplements. No human safety studies. Steroidal structure unlike other SARMs.

sarms_prohibited.reason: Unapproved SARM; potential male contraceptive; not approved for supplements
sarms_prohibited.mechanism_of_harm: One of the most potent SARMs with severe testosterone suppression (acts as male contraceptive). Causes liver toxicity, cardiovascular risks, and complete shutdown of natural testosterone production. Can cause infertility.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned in supplements (2017). Unapproved drug.
regulatory_status.Clinical_Status: Investigated as male contraceptive - causes complete testosterone suppression

sarms_prohibited.notes: One of the most potent SARMs. Completely banned from supplements. Can cause male infertility through complete testosterone suppression.

sarms_prohibited.reason: Unapproved synthetic compound; Rev-ErbA agonist; not approved for human use
sarms_prohibited.mechanism_of_harm: Rev-ErbA agonist (not a true SARM) that alters circadian rhythm and metabolism. Unapproved research chemical with no human safety data. Can cause sleep disturbances, metabolic disruption, and unknown long-term effects.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned in supplements (2017). Unapproved drug. Research chemical.
regulatory_status.WADA: Prohibited at all times (S4.5 Metabolic Modulators)

sarms_prohibited.notes: Marketed as exercise mimetic. Not a true SARM but banned similarly. Alters circadian rhythm. No human safety studies.

high_risk_ingredients.reason: Anxiety, heart palpitations, dangerous blood pressure changes
high_risk_ingredients.mechanism_of_harm: Alpha-2 adrenergic receptor antagonist that increases norepinephrine and adrenaline. Causes severe anxiety, panic attacks, rapid heart rate, dangerous blood pressure spikes, seizures, and kidney failure. Can trigger manic episodes in bipolar disorder.

regulatory_status.US: FDA warnings for serious adverse events. Not banned but high-risk.

high_risk_ingredients.notes: Can interact dangerously with antidepressants (especially MAOIs and SSRIs) and blood pressure medications. Linked to seizures, kidney failure, and death.

high_risk_ingredients.mechanism_of_harm: Contains synephrine (similar to ephedrine). Increases blood pressure and heart rate, causes arrhythmias, heart attack, and stroke. Particularly dangerous when combined with caffeine. Marketed as ephedra replacement with similar risks.

regulatory_status.US: FDA warnings for cardiovascular events. Not banned but high-risk.

high_risk_ingredients.notes: Often used as ephedra replacement. Can cause similar cardiovascular issues. Multiple case reports of heart attack and stroke, especially when combined with caffeine.

high_risk_ingredients.reason: Severe liver damage, banned in multiple countries
high_risk_ingredients.mechanism_of_harm: Contains kavalactones that cause severe hepatotoxicity including hepatitis, cirrhosis, and liver failure requiring transplantation. Mechanism unclear but may involve CYP450 enzyme inhibition and glutathione depletion. Over 100 cases of liver toxicity reported.

regulatory_status.US: FDA warnings for liver toxicity. Not banned but high-risk.

high_risk_ingredients.notes: Over 100 cases of severe liver toxicity reported including liver failure requiring transplantation. Banned in many countries. FDA consumer advisory issued.

high_risk_ingredients.reason: Liver damage at doses >800mg EGCG daily
high_risk_ingredients.mechanism_of_harm: High doses of EGCG (epigallocatechin gallate) cause hepatotoxicity through oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction. Doses >800mg daily significantly increase liver enzyme elevations and liver injury risk. Safe at normal dietary doses.

regulatory_status.US: FDA warnings for liver injury from high-dose supplements
regulatory_status.EFSA: Upper limit set at 800mg EGCG per day (2018)
regulatory_status.Canada: Health Canada warnings for high doses

high_risk_ingredients.notes: Safe at normal doses. EFSA set upper limit at 800mg EGCG per day. Liver injury risk increases significantly above this dose. Brewed tea is safe.
scientific_references: EFSA: 2018 - Safety assessment of green tea catechins

high_risk_ingredients.mechanism_of_harm: Inorganic germanium accumulates in kidneys causing irreversible renal tubular damage, kidney failure, and death. Also causes peripheral neuropathy and muscle weakness. Multiple fatalities reported. Organic germanium (Ge-132) may be safer but still controversial.

regulatory_status.US: FDA warnings. Import alerts for products from Korea.

high_risk_ingredients.notes: Multiple cases of kidney failure and death. Subject to import alerts from Korea. Inorganic forms are particularly toxic. No established nutritional benefit.

high_risk_ingredients.mechanism_of_harm: Contains mitragynine and 7-hydroxymitragynine (opioid agonists). Causes physical dependence, severe withdrawal, liver toxicity, seizures, respiratory depression, and death (especially when combined with other substances). Over 100 deaths associated with kratom.

regulatory_status.US: FDA warnings. Not lawfully marketed as supplement. DEA considering Schedule I classification. Banned in 6 states (Alabama, Arkansas, Indiana, Rhode Island, Vermont, Wisconsin).
regulatory_status.DEA: Drug of Concern. Considering Schedule I.

high_risk_ingredients.notes: Not lawfully marketed as supplement. FDA warnings ongoing. Deaths reported with combination use. Over 100 deaths associated. Highly addictive with severe withdrawal syndrome.

high_risk_ingredients.reason: Metal fibers can cause serious injury to digestive system, including perforation
high_risk_ingredients.mechanism_of_harm: Physical contamination with metal fibers from manufacturing equipment malfunction. Can cause gastrointestinal perforation, internal bleeding, choking hazard, and serious injury to digestive tract. Requires immediate medical attention if ingested.

regulatory_status.Canada: Health Canada Class I recall (July 25, 2024) - 22+ brands affected
regulatory_status.US: FDA monitoring. Class I recall risk (most serious).

high_risk_ingredients.notes: Major recalls in Canada affecting 22+ supplement brands including multivitamins, prenatal vitamins, and specialized supplements. Metal fibers originated from manufacturing equipment malfunction. Class I recall (most serious - can cause death or serious injury).
scientific_references: Health Canada Recall: July 25, 2024 - RA-84584 through RA-84605

high_risk_ingredients.reason: Lack of sterility assurance, potential bacterial contamination in compounded formulations
high_risk_ingredients.mechanism_of_harm: Bacterial contamination in non-sterile compounded GLP-1 formulations. Can cause serious infections, sepsis, abscess formation, and death. Compounding pharmacies failing sterility testing. Injectable products require strict sterility - contamination is life-threatening.

regulatory_status.US: FDA Class II recalls (2024-2025). Multiple warning letters to compounding pharmacies. FDA warns against non-FDA approved compounded versions.
regulatory_status.FDA: Ongoing enforcement against contaminated compounded GLP-1 products

high_risk_ingredients.notes: Multiple Class II recalls for contaminated compounding pharmacy GLP-1 products (2024-2025). Risk of serious infections from non-sterile preparations. FDA warning against non-FDA approved compounded versions. Only use FDA-approved products (Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro, Zepbound).
scientific_references: FDA: September 2024 - Warning on compounded semaglutide
FDA Class II Recalls: 2024-2025 - Multiple compounding pharmacies

high_risk_ingredients.reason: Potential harm to children's health, reassessment of risk-benefit profile
high_risk_ingredients.mechanism_of_harm: Excessive fluoride exposure in children causes dental fluorosis (permanent tooth discoloration and pitting), skeletal fluorosis, and potential neurodevelopmental effects. Recent studies link fluoride exposure to lower IQ in children. Risk-benefit profile no longer favorable given widespread fluoridated water.

regulatory_status.US: FDA announced plans to remove from market (May 14, 2025). Under regulatory review.
regulatory_status.NTP: National Toxicology Program (2024) - Moderate confidence that higher fluoride exposure is associated with lower IQ in children

high_risk_ingredients.notes: FDA announced plans to remove fluoride supplements for children from market following safety review (May 2025). Concerns about fluorosis and neurodevelopmental effects. NTP 2024 report found moderate confidence linking fluoride to lower IQ. Still under regulatory review.
scientific_references: NTP Monograph: August 2024 - Fluoride and neurodevelopment

high_risk_ingredients.reason: Severe hepatotoxicity; can cause liver failure and death even in small doses
high_risk_ingredients.mechanism_of_harm: Contains pulegone which is metabolized to toxic compounds causing severe liver necrosis, kidney failure, seizures, and death. Even small doses (teaspoon) can be fatal. Historically misused to induce abortion - causes maternal death before abortion occurs. Multiple fatalities documented.

regulatory_status.US: FDA warnings ongoing. Not banned but extremely dangerous.

high_risk_ingredients.notes: Especially dangerous when used to induce abortion - causes maternal death before abortion. Multiple deaths reported. Even small doses can cause liver failure. One of the most dangerous herbs.

high_risk_ingredients.reason: Severe liver damage; linked to hepatitis and liver failure
high_risk_ingredients.mechanism_of_harm: Contains nordihydroguaiaretic acid (NDGA) which causes severe hepatotoxicity including acute hepatitis, cholestatic hepatitis, cirrhosis, and liver failure requiring transplantation. At least 18 documented cases of serious liver injury. Mechanism involves oxidative stress and mitochondrial damage.

regulatory_status.US: FDA warnings since 1992. Not banned but high-risk.

high_risk_ingredients.notes: Associated with at least 18 cases of liver toxicity including cases requiring liver transplantation. FDA warning since 1992. Contains NDGA which is hepatotoxic.

high_risk_ingredients.reason: Hepatotoxicity; liver damage; multiple cases of acute hepatitis
high_risk_ingredients.mechanism_of_harm: Contains furan-containing diterpenoids that cause severe hepatotoxicity through metabolic activation to reactive intermediates. Causes acute hepatitis, chronic hepatitis, and liver failure. Over 30 cases of serious liver injury documented in France alone. Can cause death.

regulatory_status.US: FDA warnings since 1992. Not banned but high-risk.

high_risk_ingredients.notes: Banned in France, Germany, and Canada. FDA warned against use in weight loss products (1992). Over 30 cases of serious liver injury in France. Can cause liver failure and death.
scientific_references: FDA: 1992 - Warning on weight loss products

illegal_spiking_agents.mechanism_of_harm: Prescription PDE5 inhibitor illegally added to supplements. Causes dangerous blood pressure drops, especially with nitrate medications (can be fatal). Can cause heart attack, stroke, and sudden death. Most common adulterant in male enhancement supplements.

regulatory_status.US: FDA prescription drug. Illegal in supplements. Ongoing enforcement.

illegal_spiking_agents.notes: Most common adulterant in male enhancement supplements (found in 80%+ of tested products). Can be fatal with heart medications (nitrates). FDA ongoing enforcement.

illegal_spiking_agents.reason: Designer drug analog of banned tadalafil
illegal_spiking_agents.mechanism_of_harm: Designer analog of tadalafil created to evade detection. Unknown safety profile. Can cause cardiovascular events, dangerous drug interactions, and unknown long-term toxicity. No human safety studies.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned. Illegal adulterant. Found in Health Fixer products (April 2025).

illegal_spiking_agents.notes: Found in Health Fixer products (04/29/2025). Emerging designer analog. No safety data. Created to circumvent bans.

illegal_spiking_agents.reason: Designer drug analog of banned sildenafil
illegal_spiking_agents.mechanism_of_harm: Designer analog of sildenafil with unknown safety profile. Can cause cardiovascular events, dangerous blood pressure drops, and unknown toxicity. No human safety studies. Created to evade detection.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned. Illegal adulterant. Found in Health Fixer (April 2025).

illegal_spiking_agents.notes: Found in Health Fixer (04/29/2025). Designer analog to evade bans. No safety data.

illegal_spiking_agents.mechanism_of_harm: Prescription PDE5 inhibitor illegally added to supplements. Causes dangerous blood pressure drops, prolonged erections (priapism), cardiovascular events. Fatal when combined with nitrate medications.

regulatory_status.US: FDA prescription drug. Illegal in supplements.

illegal_spiking_agents.notes: Second most common adulterant in sexual enhancement products. Can cause priapism and cardiovascular events.

illegal_spiking_agents.reason: Banned weight loss drug with cardiovascular risks
illegal_spiking_agents.mechanism_of_harm: Banned weight loss drug (withdrawn 2010). Increases heart attack and stroke risk by 16%. Causes dangerous blood pressure and heart rate increases. Found illegally added to weight loss supplements.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned (October 2010). Illegal in supplements. Found in 28% of tested weight loss products.

illegal_spiking_agents.notes: Found in 28% of tested weight loss supplements despite 2010 ban. Increases cardiovascular event risk by 16%.
scientific_references: DOI: 10.1056/NEJMoa1003114 - SCOUT trial showing cardiovascular risks

illegal_spiking_agents.mechanism_of_harm: Carcinogenic laxative banned in 1999. Causes DNA damage, cancer (particularly colon cancer), and electrolyte imbalances. IARC Group 2B possible carcinogen. Still illegally added to weight loss products.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned from OTC laxatives (1999). Illegal in supplements.

illegal_spiking_agents.notes: Removed from OTC laxatives in 1999 due to cancer risk. Still found in weight loss products illegally. IARC Group 2B carcinogen.

illegal_spiking_agents.mechanism_of_harm: Prescription SSRI antidepressant illegally added to supplements. Causes serotonin syndrome (potentially fatal), severe withdrawal syndrome, suicidal ideation, and dangerous drug interactions. Requires medical supervision.

regulatory_status.US: FDA prescription drug. Illegal in supplements. Found in 21% of tested weight loss products.

illegal_spiking_agents.notes: Found in 21% of tested weight loss supplements. Can cause dangerous interactions with other antidepressants (serotonin syndrome). Severe withdrawal if stopped abruptly.

illegal_spiking_agents.mechanism_of_harm: Illegal anabolic steroids and designer analogs. Cause severe liver damage (hepatotoxicity, liver tumors), cardiovascular disease, hormone disruption, infertility, and psychiatric effects (roid rage). Often unlisted designer steroids to evade detection.

regulatory_status.US: DEA Schedule III controlled substances. Illegal in supplements. Found in 26% of tested bodybuilding products.

illegal_spiking_agents.notes: Found in 26% of tested bodybuilding supplements. Often unlisted designer steroids (methasterone, prostanozol, dimethazine). DEA Schedule III controlled substances.

illegal_spiking_agents.mechanism_of_harm: Prescription corticosteroid illegally added to supplements. Causes immunosuppression (increased infection risk), adrenal suppression (can be life-threatening if stopped abruptly), osteoporosis, diabetes, and Cushing's syndrome.

regulatory_status.US: FDA prescription drug. Illegal in supplements. Multiple recalls 2024.

illegal_spiking_agents.notes: Found in multiple recalled herbal supplements 2024. Can cause life-threatening adrenal crisis if stopped abruptly. Increases infection risk.

illegal_spiking_agents.mechanism_of_harm: Prescription NSAID illegally added to supplements. Causes cardiovascular events (heart attack, stroke), gastrointestinal bleeding, kidney damage, and liver toxicity. Black box warning for cardiovascular and GI risks.

regulatory_status.US: FDA prescription drug. Illegal in supplements. Multiple recalls 2024-2025.
regulatory_status.FDA: Black box warning for cardiovascular and GI risks

illegal_spiking_agents.notes: Found in multiple recalled supplements 2024-2025. FDA black box warning for cardiovascular and GI risks. Can cause heart attack, stroke, and GI bleeding.

illegal_spiking_agents.mechanism_of_harm: Amphetamine-like stimulant. Causes cardiovascular events, stroke (hemorrhagic stroke case reported), hypertensive crisis, and sympathomimetic toxicity. WADA prohibited. Found adulterating kratom products.

regulatory_status.US: FDA warnings. Found adulterating kratom products.

illegal_spiking_agents.notes: Found adulterating kratom products. Caused hemorrhagic stroke case (2019). WADA prohibited 2015. Amphetamine-like effects.

illegal_spiking_agents.mechanism_of_harm: Semi-synthetic opioid derivative 10-20x more potent than natural kratom alkaloids. Causes respiratory depression, overdose, death, and severe addiction. Multiple fatalities reported 2025. No safety data.

regulatory_status.US: FDA warnings (2025). Multiple deaths reported. Under DEA review.

illegal_spiking_agents.notes: New synthetic derivative (2024-2025), 10-20x more potent than natural kratom alkaloids. Multiple deaths reported 2025. Extremely dangerous.
scientific_references: FDA: 2025 warnings on synthetic kratom derivatives

illegal_spiking_agents.reason: Designer drug variants of banned tianeptine
illegal_spiking_agents.mechanism_of_harm: Designer analogs of banned tianeptine created to evade state bans. Cause opioid-like effects, severe addiction, withdrawal syndrome, and unknown toxicity. No safety data. Multiple hospitalizations 2025.

regulatory_status.US: Emerging designer drugs. Multiple states banning. FDA warnings.
regulatory_status.States: Being added to state bans as identified

illegal_spiking_agents.notes: New designer variants emerging to circumvent state bans (2025). Multiple hospitalizations 2025. No safety data. Highly addictive.

heavy_metal_limits.mechanism_of_harm: Neurotoxic heavy metal. No safe level of exposure. Causes irreversible brain damage, cognitive impairment, developmental delays in children, kidney damage, and cardiovascular disease. Accumulates in bones. California Prop 65 limit (0.5 mcg/day) much stricter than FDA (10 mcg/day).

regulatory_status.US: FDA limit: 10 mcg/day. California Prop 65: 0.5 mcg/day (reproductive toxin).
regulatory_status.WHO: No safe level of lead exposure

heavy_metal_limits.notes: California Prop 65 limit much stricter (0.5 vs 10 mcg/day). Common in herbal supplements from China/India, bone meal calcium, Ayurvedic supplements. No safe level of exposure per WHO.
scientific_references: DOI: 10.1016/S0140-6736(18)31310-2 - Lancet: Global burden of lead exposure
heavy_metal_limits.reason: Neurotoxic; harmful to brain development and cognitive function. Prop 65 limit: 0.5 mcg/day

heavy_metal_limits.mechanism_of_harm: IARC Group 1 carcinogen (inorganic arsenic). Causes skin, lung, bladder, and liver cancer. Causes skin lesions, cardiovascular disease, diabetes, and neurotoxicity. Inorganic arsenic (arsenite, arsenate) much more toxic than organic forms. Common in rice-based products.

regulatory_status.US: FDA limit: 10 mcg/day (varies by product). FDA action level for apple juice: 10 ppb.
regulatory_status.Recent: Zaarah Herbals recall (March 2025) for arsenic contamination

heavy_metal_limits.notes: Inorganic arsenic (arsenite, arsenate) more toxic than organic forms. Common in rice-based products, seaweed, herbal supplements. Recalled in Zaarah Herbals products 2025. IARC Group 1 carcinogen.
heavy_metal_limits.reason: Carcinogenic; linked to skin, lung, and bladder cancer. FDA concern level varies by product type

heavy_metal_limits.health_effects: Kidney damage, bone disease, lung damage
heavy_metal_limits.mechanism_of_harm: IARC Group 1 carcinogen. Accumulates in kidneys causing kidney damage and failure. Causes bone disease (osteoporosis, osteomalacia), lung damage, and cancer. Long biological half-life (10-30 years). Higher levels in chocolate, cacao, and zinc supplements.

regulatory_status.US: FDA limit: 5 mcg/day. USP limit: 5 mcg/day.

heavy_metal_limits.notes: Accumulates in kidneys (half-life 10-30 years). Higher levels in chocolate, cacao, and zinc supplements. IARC Group 1 carcinogen. Causes kidney damage and bone disease.
heavy_metal_limits.reason: Kidney damage; bone loss. California Prop 65 limit: 4.1 mcg/day

heavy_metal_limits.mechanism_of_harm: Neurotoxic heavy metal. Methylmercury most toxic form - crosses blood-brain barrier and placenta. Causes severe neurotoxicity, developmental delays in children, cognitive impairment, kidney damage, and cardiovascular disease. Extremely harmful to developing fetuses. Fish oil should be molecularly distilled to remove mercury.

regulatory_status.US: FDA limit: 15 mcg/day. EPA reference dose: 0.1 mcg/kg/day (methylmercury).

heavy_metal_limits.notes: Methylmercury most toxic form (crosses blood-brain barrier). Fish oil should be molecularly distilled. High levels in some TCM products, unpurified fish oil, shark cartilage. California Prop 65 limit (0.3 mcg/day) much stricter than FDA (15 mcg/day).
heavy_metal_limits.reason: Neurotoxic; harmful to developing fetuses and children. EPA reference dose: 0.1 mcg/kg/day

wada_prohibited_2024.mechanism_of_harm: Opioid analgesic prohibited in athletic competition by WADA (2024). Causes respiratory depression, addiction, and performance enhancement concerns. 24-hour washout period required before competition.

regulatory_status.US: FDA prescription drug (Schedule IV controlled substance)

wada_prohibited_2024.notes: New addition to 2024 WADA Prohibited List. In-competition only. 24-hour washout period required. DEA Schedule IV controlled substance.
wada_prohibited_2024.reason: Prohibited by World Anti-Doping Agency (WADA) for athletic competitions

wada_prohibited_2024.mechanism_of_harm: Cannabinoid prohibited in athletic competition by WADA. Threshold: 150 ng/mL urine. CBD not prohibited if THC content <0.3%. In-competition only (not prohibited out-of-competition).

regulatory_status.WADA: Prohibited in-competition only. Threshold: 150 ng/mL urine.
regulatory_status.US: DEA Schedule I (federal). State laws vary. CBD legal if THC <0.3%.
regulatory_status.Note: CBD not prohibited by WADA if THC <0.3%

wada_prohibited_2024.notes: In-competition only (not prohibited out-of-competition). CBD not prohibited if THC <0.3%. Threshold: 150 ng/mL urine. Athletes should verify THC content in CBD products.
wada_prohibited_2024.reason: Prohibited by World Anti-Doping Agency (WADA) for athletic competitions

state_regional_bans.mechanism_of_harm: Opioid-like botanical with addiction potential. Contains mitragynine and 7-hydroxymitragynine (opioid receptor agonists). Causes addiction, withdrawal syndrome, liver toxicity, and deaths (often in combination with other substances). State-level bans expanding. Federal scheduling under DEA consideration.

regulatory_status.US_Federal: Not federally scheduled. FDA warnings. DEA considering scheduling.
regulatory_status.States_Banned: Alabama, Arkansas, Indiana, Rhode Island, Vermont, Wisconsin

state_regional_bans.notes: Louisiana Schedule I effective Aug 1, 2025. Federal scheduling under DEA consideration. 6 states currently ban. FDA issued multiple warnings. Opioid-like effects and addiction potential.

state_regional_bans.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic stimulants (DMAA and DMHA) with amphetamine-like effects. Cause cardiovascular events (heart attack, stroke, sudden death), hypertension, and psychiatric effects. Multiple deaths reported. State-level bans expanding beyond federal FDA action. Includes all analogs.

regulatory_status.US_Federal: FDA banned from supplements. Not approved as dietary ingredient.

state_regional_bans.notes: State-level bans expanding beyond federal FDA action (2025). California effective Aug 1, New York effective Sept 1. Includes all analogs. Multiple deaths reported. DoD prohibited.
scientific_references: FDA: DMAA not a dietary ingredient

state_regional_bans.reason: Psychoactive cannabinoid, safety concerns, lack of regulation
state_regional_bans.mechanism_of_harm: Psychoactive cannabinoid typically synthesized from CBD through chemical conversion. Produces intoxicating effects similar to delta-9 THC. Safety concerns include: unregulated manufacturing with potential contaminants, unknown long-term effects, inconsistent potency, and psychoactive effects affecting driving/cognition. Can cause anxiety, paranoia, and rapid heart rate.

regulatory_status.US_Federal: Legal loophole via 2018 Farm Bill (hemp-derived). DEA position unclear. FY2026 Appropriations Act may affect status.
regulatory_status.FDA: No approved use. Warning letters issued.

state_regional_bans.notes: Psychoactive cannabinoid banned in 15+ states. Synthesized from CBD. Produces intoxicating effects. Unregulated manufacturing raises safety concerns. State bans expanding. FY2026 Congressional action may affect federal status.
scientific_references: FDA: 5 Things to Know about Delta-8 THC

new_emerging_threats.mechanism_of_harm: Emerging threat: AI-designed synthetic compounds with completely unknown safety profiles, toxicity, or long-term effects. No human safety studies. Unregulated. Can evade detection. Potential for severe adverse effects.

regulatory_status.US: FDA monitoring. No specific regulations yet. Emerging threat.

new_emerging_threats.notes: Emerging threat (2025) of AI-designed compounds with unknown safety profiles. No human studies. Completely unregulated. High risk.
scientific_references: FDA: Emerging threats in dietary supplements

new_emerging_threats.mechanism_of_harm: Deliberate or accidental substitution of safe botanicals with toxic plants. Yellow oleander (cardiac glycosides - fatal) substituted for tejocote root. Can cause death, cardiac arrest, and severe toxicity. Class I recalls (most serious). FDA expanding investigations.

regulatory_status.US: FDA Class I recalls. Expanding investigations. Multiple warnings 2024-2025.
regulatory_status.FDA: Class I recall level - most serious

new_emerging_threats.notes: Yellow oleander in tejocote root (2024-2025). Expanding FDA investigations. Class I recalls (most serious - can cause death). Multiple fatalities.

new_emerging_threats.reason: Unknown safety profile, potential endocrine disruption
new_emerging_threats.mechanism_of_harm: Novel synthetic peptides and growth factors with no safety data. Potential for severe endocrine disruption, cancer risk, organ damage, and unknown long-term effects. No human safety studies. Increasing presence in bodybuilding supplements.

regulatory_status.US: FDA warnings. Not approved as dietary ingredients. Illegal in supplements.

new_emerging_threats.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic compounds fraudulently sold as natural bacopa extract. Unknown toxicity of synthetic compounds. Fraud investigation 2025 found synthetic compounds in 'natural' bacopa products. No safety data on synthetic adulterants.

new_emerging_threats.notes: Investigation found synthetic compounds in 'natural' bacopa products (2025). Fraud. Unknown toxicity of synthetic adulterants.

Causes serotonin syndrome (potentially fatal), dangerous drug interactions with other antidepressants, psychiatric effects, and requires medical supervision. Found in 15% of tested products.

regulatory_status.US: FDA prescription drug. Illegal in supplements. Found in 15% of tested male enhancement products (2025).

FDA requested voluntary withdrawal after clinical trial showed increased cancer incidence. Still illegally added to weight loss supplements. Causes cancer, psychiatric effects, and cardiovascular issues.

regulatory_status.US: FDA requested market withdrawal (February 13, 2020) due to cancer risk. Illegal in supplements. Still found in products (2025).

Withdrawn from EU market 2008 due to severe psychiatric side effects (depression, anxiety, suicidal ideation). Still illegally added to weight loss supplements. Causes severe psychiatric effects and suicide risk.

pharmaceutical_adulterants.reason: Prescription diabetes medication illegally added to weight loss supplements
pharmaceutical_adulterants.mechanism_of_harm: Prescription diabetes medication (biguanide) illegally added to weight loss supplements. Causes dangerous hypoglycemia (low blood sugar - can be fatal), lactic acidosis (potentially fatal), and vitamin B12 deficiency. Requires medical monitoring. Found in numerous recalled weight loss products.

designer_stimulants.mechanism_of_harm: Dangerous synthetic cathinone (designer stimulant). Causes severe psychosis, extreme addiction, cardiovascular toxicity, hyperthermia, and death. Similar to bath salts. Found in pre-workout supplements labeled as 'proprietary blend'. No safety data.

regulatory_status.US: DEA Schedule I (controlled substance). Illegal in supplements.

designer_stimulants.notes: Found in pre-workout supplements labeled as 'proprietary blend'. DEA Schedule I controlled substance. Causes severe psychosis and addiction.

designer_stimulants.mechanism_of_harm: Designer analogs of banned DMAA/DMHA created to evade bans. Cause heart attacks, strokes, hypertension, and unknown long-term effects. No safety data. Amphetamine-like effects. New analogs constantly emerging.

designer_stimulants.notes: New analogs designed to evade DMAA/DMHA bans (2025). Cause heart attacks and strokes. No safety data. State-level bans expanding.

synthetic_cannabinoids.mechanism_of_harm: Dangerous synthetic cannabinoid (designer drug). Causes seizures, psychosis, death, kidney damage, and severe toxicity. Much more potent than natural THC. Found in products marketed as legal CBD alternatives. No safety data.

regulatory_status.US: DEA Schedule I (controlled substance). Illegal in supplements.

synthetic_cannabinoids.notes: Found in products marketed as legal CBD alternatives. DEA Schedule I controlled substance. Causes seizures, psychosis, and death. Much more potent than natural THC.

schedule_I_psychoactives.reason: DEA Schedule I controlled substance - high potential for abuse, no accepted medical use
schedule_I_psychoactives.mechanism_of_harm: Serotonin 5-HT2A receptor agonist causing profound alterations in perception, cognition, and mood. Can trigger severe psychological distress, psychosis, dangerous behavior, HPPD (persistent perceptual changes), and serotonin syndrome when combined with other serotonergic substances. FDA has NOT approved for any medical use despite 'breakthrough therapy' designations for research.

regulatory_status.US: DEA Schedule I controlled substance. Illegal in dietary supplements. FDA issued 50+ warning letters in 2024-2025 for products containing or implying psilocybin.
regulatory_status.DEA: Schedule I since 1970. Possession is federal felony.
regulatory_status.Oregon: Legalized supervised use (2020), NOT legal in supplements.
regulatory_status.Colorado: Decriminalized personal use (2022), NOT legal in supplements.
regulatory_status.Global: Schedule I equivalent in most countries (UN Convention)

schedule_I_psychoactives.notes: FDA issued 50+ warning letters in 2024-2025 for products implying psilocybin content. Often disguised as 'functional mushroom blends' or 'adaptogenic mushrooms'. Some products contain actual psilocybin; others make false psychedelic claims. Either case is illegal. State decriminalization does NOT make it legal in supplements.
scientific_references: FDA: Warning letters to psilocybin product sellers (2024-2025)

schedule_I_psychoactives.reason: Toxic muscimol causing seizures, hospitalizations, and death - Diamond Shruumz recall 2024
schedule_I_psychoactives.mechanism_of_harm: Contains muscimol (GABA-A agonist) and ibotenic acid (neurotoxin). Causes unpredictable psychoactive effects, seizures, coma, respiratory depression, and death. Diamond Shruumz products caused 39 illnesses, 1 death across 20 states (2024). Toxic dose close to psychoactive dose - extreme overdose risk.

regulatory_status.US: FDA Class I recalls (2024-ongoing). Import alerts active. Not approved as dietary ingredient.
regulatory_status.FDA: Multiple warning letters. Not legal to sell as supplement.

schedule_I_psychoactives.notes: Diamond Shruumz recall (June 2024) caused 39 illnesses, 1 death, seizures across 20 states. Louisiana banned Aug 2025. Marketed as 'legal shrooms' or 'legal psychedelic'. Toxic dose unpredictable. Often contaminated or misdosed. NOT safe as dietary ingredient.
scientific_references: FDA: Diamond Shruumz Class I Recall (2024)

novel_peptides.mechanism_of_harm: Unapproved research peptide with no human safety data. Potential for cancer promotion (angiogenesis), immune system disruption, and unknown long-term effects. Not approved as dietary ingredient. Increasingly found in 'healing' supplements (2025).

regulatory_status.US: FDA: Not approved as dietary ingredient. Illegal in supplements.
regulatory_status.WADA: Prohibited at all times (S0 - Non-approved substances)
regulatory_status.FDA: Warning letters issued to companies selling TB-500

novel_peptides.notes: Increasingly found in 'healing' supplements 2025. No human safety data. WADA prohibited. Potential cancer promotion risk (angiogenesis).

novel_peptides.mechanism_of_harm: Unapproved research peptide with no human safety data. Potential cancer risks, hormone disruption, and unknown safety profile. No oral bioavailability (ineffective orally). Not approved as dietary ingredient. Commonly found in oral supplements despite being ineffective orally.

regulatory_status.US: FDA: Not approved as dietary ingredient. Illegal in supplements.
regulatory_status.WADA: Prohibited at all times (S0 - Non-approved substances)

novel_peptides.notes: Commonly found in oral supplements despite no oral bioavailability (ineffective orally). No human safety data. WADA prohibited. Potential cancer and hormone disruption risks.
scientific_references: WADA: Prohibited List - S0 Non-approved substances

novel_peptides.reason: Unapproved drug with serious safety concerns including melanoma risk
novel_peptides.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic analog of alpha-melanocyte-stimulating hormone (α-MSH). Stimulates melanin production causing skin darkening. Causes nausea, facial flushing, spontaneous erections (priapism), cardiovascular effects, and dangerous mole changes. Major concern: may promote or mask melanoma development. No quality control in black market products. Injection-related infection risks.

regulatory_status.US: FDA unapproved drug. Warning letters issued. Illegal to sell for human use.
regulatory_status.UK: MHRA warnings issued. Illegal to sell.
regulatory_status.EU: Not approved. Illegal in most countries.

novel_peptides.notes: Known as 'Barbie Drug'. Sold illegally online and in tanning salons. Causes skin to tan without sun exposure. Major concerns: mole darkening/changes may mask melanoma, product contamination, injection site infections. WADA prohibited (S0 - Non-approved substances). Reports of priapism requiring medical intervention.
scientific_references: FDA Warning: Melanotan II - Unapproved tanning drug
WADA: Prohibited List S0 - Non-approved substances

novel_peptides.reason: GHB precursor - metabolizes to date rape drug, causes overdose and death
novel_peptides.mechanism_of_harm: Industrial solvent that is metabolized by alcohol dehydrogenase and aldehyde dehydrogenase into gamma-hydroxybutyrate (GHB), a Schedule I controlled substance. Causes CNS depression, respiratory depression, unconsciousness, seizures, coma, and death. Synergistic with alcohol - extremely dangerous combination. Used as date rape drug precursor.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned in dietary supplements. DEA List I chemical. Federal Analogue Act applies when sold for human consumption. Schedule I controlled substance analogue.
regulatory_status.DEA: List I chemical (GHB precursor). Schedule I analogue when intended for human consumption.
regulatory_status.States: Controlled in many states as GHB analogue

novel_peptides.notes: EXTREMELY DANGEROUS. Converts to GHB in body. Previously sold in supplements marketed for bodybuilding, sleep, and 'euphoria'. Multiple deaths reported. Dangerous with alcohol (blocks metabolism, prolongs effects). Products sold as 'Revitalize Plus', 'Serenity', 'Enliven', 'GHRE'. FDA/DEA joint enforcement actions.
scientific_references: FDA: Warning - GHB-related products in dietary supplements

research_chemicals.mechanism_of_harm: Designer analogs of cardarine (GW501516) created to evade bans. Original cardarine caused cancer in animal studies (multiple organ tumors). Analogs have unknown toxicity but likely similar cancer risk. No human safety data. New derivatives constantly emerging.

regulatory_status.US: FDA banned. Not approved as dietary ingredient. Illegal in supplements.
regulatory_status.WADA: Prohibited at all times (S4 - Hormone and metabolic modulators)

research_chemicals.notes: New derivatives emerging to evade WADA/FDA bans (2025). Original cardarine caused cancer in animal studies. Analogs likely have similar cancer risk. No human safety data.
scientific_references: WADA: Prohibited List - S4 Hormone and metabolic modulators

recalled_supplement_brands.reason: Linked to liver damage, jaundice, and one death. Multiple recalls.
recalled_supplement_brands.mechanism_of_harm: Weight loss supplement linked to severe liver damage (hepatotoxicity), jaundice, liver failure, and one death. FDA recall in May 2009. Reformulated products still associated with adverse events. Exact causative ingredient unclear.

regulatory_status.US: FDA recall (May 2009). Reformulated products on market but still associated with adverse events.

recalled_supplement_brands.notes: Recalled in 2009 (23 reports of liver damage, 1 death). Reformulated but still associated with adverse events. Exact causative ingredient unclear.

recalled_supplement_brands.reason: Acute liver failure; dozens of hospitalizations; one death; one liver transplant
recalled_supplement_brands.mechanism_of_harm: Weight loss supplement linked to outbreak of acute non-viral hepatitis in Hawaii (2013). Caused acute liver failure, dozens of hospitalizations, one death, one liver transplant. Contained aegeline (banned ingredient). Permanently recalled.

regulatory_status.US: FDA recall (November 8, 2013). Permanently banned. Class I recall (most serious).

recalled_supplement_brands.notes: Linked to outbreak of acute non-viral hepatitis in Hawaii (2013). 56 cases of liver injury, 3 liver transplants, 1 death. Contained aegeline (banned). Permanently recalled. Class I recall.

recalled_supplement_brands.reason: Contained DMAA; linked to heart attacks and deaths in military personnel
recalled_supplement_brands.mechanism_of_harm: Pre-workout supplement containing DMAA (banned stimulant). Linked to heart attacks, strokes, and deaths in military personnel (2 US soldiers died). Caused cardiovascular events. Original formula banned. Reformulated without DMAA.

regulatory_status.US: FDA: Original formula banned (2012). Reformulated without DMAA.

recalled_supplement_brands.notes: Original formula banned (2012). Linked to deaths of two US soldiers. Contained DMAA (banned stimulant). Reformulated without DMAA. DoD banned from military bases.

recalled_supplement_brands.reason: Contains undeclared trendione (anabolic steroid); linked to consumer stroke
recalled_supplement_brands.mechanism_of_harm: FDA laboratory testing found undeclared trendione (estra-4,9,11-triene-3,17-dione), a Schedule III controlled anabolic steroid. Consumer reported adverse event: stroke. Trendione causes liver toxicity, testosterone suppression, cardiovascular events including heart attack and stroke.

regulatory_status.US: FDA alert December 2, 2025. Consumers urged not to use. Anabolic steroid Schedule III controlled substance.
regulatory_status.DEA: Trendione is Schedule III controlled substance

recalled_supplement_brands.notes: FDA alert December 2, 2025 based on adverse event report - consumer suffered stroke after use. Contains undeclared Schedule III anabolic steroid trendione. Part of ongoing FDA enforcement against bodybuilding products containing hidden anabolic steroids.
scientific_references: FDA Alert: December 2, 2025 - GE Labs Ykarine contains hidden drug

recalled_supplement_brands.reason: Extreme lead contamination - up to 11,100 ppm lead
recalled_supplement_brands.mechanism_of_harm: FDA testing found lead levels up to 11,100 ppm (parts per million). Lead is a toxic heavy metal with no safe level. Causes neurological damage, kidney damage, cardiovascular effects, reproductive harm. Particularly dangerous for children, pregnant women. Chronic exposure causes irreversible CNS damage.

regulatory_status.US: FDA recall December 2025. Consumers advised to stop use immediately. Class I recall.

recalled_supplement_brands.notes: EXTREME lead contamination - 11,100 ppm lead detected. For context, FDA action level for lead in candy is 0.1 ppm. Two lots recalled. Joint pain/arthritis supplement. Consumers should stop use immediately and contact healthcare provider.
scientific_references: FDA Recall: December 25-29, 2025 - Rheumacare Capsules lead contamination

recalled_supplement_brands.reason: Contains undeclared sildenafil and tadalafil (prescription erectile dysfunction drugs)
recalled_supplement_brands.mechanism_of_harm: FDA laboratory analysis found undeclared sildenafil and tadalafil (active ingredients in Viagra and Cialis). These interact dangerously with nitrate medications for blood pressure/heart disease, causing potentially fatal drop in blood pressure. Particularly dangerous for cardiac patients.

regulatory_status.US: FDA public notification November 21, 2025. StuffbyNainax LLC voluntary nationwide recall. Distributed August-November 2025 online including eBay.

recalled_supplement_brands.notes: Male enhancement supplement with hidden prescription drugs sildenafil/tadalafil. Distributed nationwide online August-November 2025. Life-threatening for people taking nitrates for cardiac conditions. Part of ongoing FDA enforcement against tainted sexual enhancement products.
scientific_references: FDA Public Notification: November 21, 2025 - MR.7 SUPER 700000 hidden drugs
FDA Recall: StuffbyNainax LLC voluntary recall

recalled_supplement_brands.reason: Illegally marketing products containing SARMs as dietary supplements
recalled_supplement_brands.mechanism_of_harm: SARMs are unapproved drugs associated with serious safety concerns including liver toxicity, cardiovascular events (heart attack, stroke), testosterone suppression, and unknown long-term effects. FDA issued warning letter December 12, 2025.

regulatory_status.US: FDA warning letter December 12, 2025. Products are 'new drugs' under FD&C Act, not dietary supplements.
regulatory_status.FDA: Warning letter - products marketed as supplements are unapproved drugs

recalled_supplement_brands.notes: FDA warning letter December 12, 2025. SARMs marketed to teens and young adults via social media influencers. SARMs cause liver damage, heart attack, stroke risk. Not approved for any human use. Marketed illegally as dietary supplements.
scientific_references: FDA Warning Letter: TITAN SARMS LLC - December 12, 2025 (719645)

_metadata.description: Banned and recalled ingredients database for regulatory compliance and safety

version_2.0_fields: CUI - UMLS Concept Unique Identifier (C followed by 7 digits, or empty string)
mechanism_of_harm - Detailed explanation of how substance causes harm
regulatory_status - Jurisdiction-specific regulatory information (US, FDA, EU, Canada, WADA, DEA, etc.)
ban_effective_date - Specific effective date of ban (YYYY-MM-DD format)
scientific_references - Array of DOI citations, FDA warnings, and regulatory documents
Expanded aliases - 200+ additional aliases for robust ingredient matching
version_2.2_fields: severity_score - Integer 1-10 for sorting (10 = most severe)
interaction_tags - Structured list for drug interaction checking

cross_file_changes.harmful_additives.json: Removed Red No. 3 (ADD_RED3) - now completely banned, belongs only in banned_recalled_ingredients.json. Updated metadata to 96 entries.

_metadata.note: Update 2026-01-05: Added 5 new recalls (GE Labs Ykarine, Rheumacare, MR.7 SUPER 700000, TITAN SARMS). Updated Yellow Oleander (26 products), 7-OH (Schedule I recommendation), CBD (Congressional/Executive action). NMN status: FDA reversed Sep 2025, now legal in supplements. Schema 2.1, 94 total entries.
_metadata.nmn_status_note: IMPORTANT: NMN (Nicotinamide Mononucleotide) was previously excluded from dietary supplements by FDA drug exclusion rule (Nov 2022). FDA REVERSED this decision September 2025, concluding NMN was marketed as supplement before drug investigation (2017). NMN is now LEGAL in dietary supplements in the US. This reversal may set precedent for CBD pathway.
